co_catalyst.name: various metals (Cu, Ag, Pt, Pd, etc.)
co_catalyst.role: electron trap and charge separation enhancement

additives.name: NaOH
additives.role: hole scavenger and CO2 solubility enhancer

chemistry.product: CH4, CH3OH, CO, HCHO, C2H4, C2H6, HCOOH
chemistry.scope_summary: Review covers various TiO2-based photocatalysts (pure, metal-doped, non-metal doped, co-doped, semiconductor coupled, dye-sensitized) for CO2 reduction with H2O to produce various hydrocarbon fuels and chemicals under UV and visible light irradiation in different reactor configurations

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Schematic of TiO2 photocatalyzed reaction where CB and VB represents the conduction band, and valence band, respectively. | Fig. 2. Band gap of some photocatalysts with respect to the redox potential of different chemical species measured at pH of 7. Adapted from Refs. [35–38].

Fig. 1. Schematic of TiO2 photocatalyzed reaction where CB and VB represents the conduction band, and valence band, respectively. | Fig. 2. Band gap of some photocatalysts with respect to the redox potential of different chemical species measured at pH of 7. Adapted from Refs. [35–38].
Fig. 3. Excitation steps with a photosensitizer, where A and D represent the electron acceptor and electron donor, respectively.

key_observations_supporting_mechanism: Electrons in conduction band (CB) participate in reduction reactions
Holes in valence band (VB) participate in oxidation reactions
figures.caption: Fig. 4. Coupling of TiO2 with semiconductors (SC) illustrating direct (A) and indirect (B) Z-scheme.
Fig. 5. Two dimensional representation of a single TiO2 crystal lattice showing substitutional and interstitial doping.

Fig. 6. Band structure of titania (a) before doping and (b) after doping where Eg, Ef, F, x, and s represent the band gap energy, Fermi level, work function, electron affinity and semiconductor. Reprinted from Ref. [134] with permission.

Fig. 7. Band structure of titania (a) before contact and (b) after contact with a metal, where the Schottky barrier is formed. (Fm and Ef int represent the metal work function and Fermi level if titania is an intrinsic semiconductor respectively. Reprinted from Ref. [134] with permission. | Fig. 8. Metal modified semiconductor photocatalyst.

Fig. 7. Band structure of titania (a) before contact and (b) after contact with a metal, where the Schottky barrier is formed. (Fm and Ef int represent the metal work function and Fermi level if titania is an intrinsic semiconductor respectively. Reprinted from Ref. [134] with permission. | Fig. 8. Metal modified semiconductor photocatalyst.

figures.flow_path: CO2 + H2O enters through incident light rays, passes through catalyst coated monolith structure, exits as fuels
figures.reactor_geometry: monolithic structure with triangular/honeycomb internal geometry
figures.light_source_position: side emitting optical fiber positioned externally
figures.key_information: photocatalytic reactor with catalyst coated monolith walls, light delivery via side emitting optical fiber with core and cladding, converts CO2 + H2O to fuels using reflected and refracted light rays
figures.caption: Fig. 9. Schematic of light propagation in a single channel of a coated honeycomb monolith threaded with a non-coated side light emitting optical fiber.

figures.flow_path: inlet to reactor chamber to outlet
figures.key_information: Three different photoreactor configurations shown: (A) suspended catalyst in aqueous medium, (B) catalyst coated optical fibres, (C) catalyst coated monolithic channels with light propagating optical fibres
figures.caption: Fig. 10. Schematic of (A) slurry reactor design with top illumination, (B) optical fiber reactor design with side illumination and (C) internally illuminated reactor with top illumination.

figure_descriptions: Photocatalytic reactor with catalyst coated monolith walls and optical fiber light delivery
Multiple reactor configurations including slurry, optical fiber, and internally illuminated designs

notes: This is a review paper focused on CO2 photoreduction over TiO2 catalysts. No specific batch vs flow comparison data was available. The paper primarily discusses various reactor configurations and catalyst designs for photocatalytic CO2 reduction. Confidence is low due to lack of quantitative experimental data typical of review papers.
